event: Mesolithic hunter-gatherers leave the earliest evidence of human activity on Guernsey as rising seas separate the island from mainland Europe.
Neolithic farmers settle the island and build megalithic passage graves and dolmens such as Le Déhus and La Varde.
The carved granite statue-menhir La Gran'mère du Chimquière is erected, reflecting the island's prehistoric ritual culture.
Roman influence reaches the Channel Islands following Julius Caesar's conquest of Gaul, and Guernsey enters the orbit of Roman trade.
A Gallo-Roman merchant ship, later excavated in St Peter Port harbour as 'Asterix', sinks while trading along the island's coast.
Saint Sampson lands on Guernsey to evangelise the island, an event commemorated in the parish and harbour that bear his name.
Guernsey and the Channel Islands are annexed to the Duchy of Normandy under Duke William Longsword.
Duke William of Normandy conquers England, linking Guernsey's Norman rulers to the English crown.
King John loses mainland Normandy to France, and the Channel Islands choose to remain loyal to the English crown as self-governing possessions.
The Treaty of Paris formally cedes mainland Normandy to France while confirming the islands as a dependency of the English crown.
Construction begins on Castle Cornet, the island's principal fortress guarding St Peter Port harbour.
French forces invade and occupy parts of Guernsey during the early Hundred Years' War, seizing Castle Cornet.
Owain Lawgoch leads a Franco-Welsh raid on Guernsey, fought at the legendary Battle of La Descente des Aragousais.
A Papal Bull of Neutrality grants the Channel Islands protected status, sparing them from attack during Anglo-French conflicts.
Three women are burned at the stake in St Peter Port during the Marian persecutions, the island's most infamous religious executions.
Queen Elizabeth I grants a royal charter confirming Guernsey's traditional rights and the authority of its Royal Court.
Guernsey sides with Parliament in the English Civil War while royalist Castle Cornet holds out under siege for nearly nine years.
Castle Cornet surrenders to Parliamentary forces, ending the longest siege of the English Civil War.
A lightning strike ignites the gunpowder magazine at Castle Cornet, destroying its medieval keep.
Guernsey privateers flourish, preying on enemy shipping and helping to build the island's maritime wealth.
Privateering and trade make Guernsey prosperous, with St Peter Port emerging as a thriving Georgian port town.
Fort George is completed above St Peter Port as a major British garrison amid the wars with Revolutionary France.
The end of the Napoleonic Wars cuts off privateering income, prompting Guernsey to shift toward horticulture and granite quarrying.
Guernsey's distinctive cattle breed gains formal recognition, becoming a prized export valued for its rich milk.
Steamship services connect Guernsey regularly to England, boosting trade and the export of produce and stone.
Victor Hugo arrives in exile and later settles at Hauteville House, where he writes much of his major work including Les Misérables.
Victor Hugo publishes 'Les Travailleurs de la mer' (Toilers of the Sea), set in and dedicated to Guernsey.
Tomatoes grown under glass become Guernsey's dominant export crop, shaping the island's economy for decades.
Guernseymen serve in the First World War, including in the Royal Guernsey Light Infantry raised for the conflict.
The Royal Guernsey Light Infantry suffers heavy casualties at the Battle of Cambrai on the Western Front.
Sterling currency is given parity with Guernsey's local money as the island modernises its finances.
German forces occupy Guernsey, the only part of the British Isles to fall under Nazi control during the Second World War.
Hundreds of islanders are deported to internment camps in Germany under occupation policy.
Following the D-Day landings the German garrison is cut off, and islanders endure severe shortages until Red Cross relief arrives via the SS Vega.
Guernsey is liberated on 9 May, now celebrated annually as Liberation Day, the island's national holiday.
Major constitutional reform reorganises the States of Guernsey, replacing Crown-appointed jurats with elected deputies in government.
Alderney's States is reconstituted and links closely with Guernsey for many public services after wartime evacuation.
Postwar rebuilding accelerates as tourism grows rapidly alongside the island's horticulture industry.
Guernsey establishes itself as an offshore finance centre, with banking and insurance becoming pillars of the economy.
Through the United Kingdom's accession to the European Community, Guernsey gains a special relationship under Protocol 3 without full membership.
Guernsey strengthens financial regulation to meet international standards as its finance industry matures.
Guernsey signs tax information exchange agreements, moving off international 'grey lists' and reinforcing its reputation as a finance centre.
The United Kingdom's Brexit referendum ends Guernsey's Protocol 3 relationship with the EU, prompting new arrangements with Europe.
Guernsey holds its first island-wide general election, replacing district-based voting with a single constituency for all deputies.
Guernsey's borders close during the COVID-19 pandemic, and an effective elimination strategy keeps cases extremely low.
